question: Does the output include content for all four specified instruments (piano, violin, flute, timpani) without omitting any?
Does the output contain at least 8 distinct visualizations, specifically one spectrum diagram AND one waveform graph for each of the four instruments?
Does the text consistently use A4 (440 Hz) as the reference pitch for all instrument explanations and visualizations?
Does the text accurately explain the physical causes of inharmonicity or non-harmonic overtones for the Piano (string stiffness) and Timpani (circular membrane modes)?
Does the Flute's spectrum diagram show a strong peak at the fundamental (440 Hz) with significantly fewer or weaker higher harmonics compared to the Violin's spectrum?
Does the Violin's spectrum diagram display a rich harmonic series with prominent energy extending into higher integer harmonics (both odd and even)?
Does the spectrum diagram for the Timpani (or Piano) visually depict or label non-integer/inharmonic partials, distinguishing it from the perfect harmonic series of the Violin/Flute?
Do the waveform graphs correctly contrast the instruments: showing a smooth/sinusoidal shape for the Flute versus a complex/jagged shape for the Violin?
Are all generated charts clearly labeled with legible axes: 'Frequency (Hz)'/Amplitude for spectra and 'Time (ms)'/Amplitude for waveforms?
Do all spectrum diagrams use a consistent frequency scale (range) and do all waveform graphs use a consistent time scale to allow for valid visual comparison across instruments?
Are the visualizations placed immediately adjacent to the relevant text sections describing that specific instrument's sound characteristics?
Does the text explicitly reference the generated images using transitional phrases (e.g., 'As seen in the spectrum below...') to integrate the visuals into the explanation?
Does the content logically derive subjective timbre descriptors (e.g., 'warm,' 'pure,' 'percussive') from the objective physical data shown in the generated spectra and waveforms?
